marcus_scaurus_convo_2_n01.npcDialogue:
A slave delivers a sealed letter. The wax bears the seal of the Princeps Senatus — Scaurus's seal. The slave tells you the old man died three days ago in his bed. He left instructions that this letter be delivered to you when Roman legions entered the city gates under a general's command. The seal is unbroken. You open it.

"If you are reading this, then what I feared has come to pass, and I was not strong enough to outlive it. I confess I am grateful for that small mercy.

I have spent my life in service to a system that I believed was greater than any man within it. I defended that belief in the senate, in the courts, in private counsel with every consul and censor I outlived. I defended it when the Gracchi fell. I defended it when Saturninus fell. I defended it when Drusus fell. Each time, I told myself: the system endures. The system corrects. The system is stronger than the men who assault it.

I was wrong.

The system was never stronger than the men within it. It was exactly as strong as their willingness to be bound by it — no more, no less. And we have raised a generation of men who are willing to be bound by nothing."

responses.playerDialogue: You continue reading, each line heavier than the last.
Your hands tighten on the parchment. You read on.
The old man's words cut deeper than you expected. You keep reading.
You recognize the precision of a man who has had time to choose every word. You read the rest.

marcus_scaurus_convo_2_n02.npcDialogue:
"Do not mourn the Republic as though it were murdered. It was not. Murder requires an assailant and a victim. What happened to Rome is closer to what happens to a house when everyone agrees to stop maintaining the roof: the rain comes in gradually, then all at once, and by the time the ceiling collapses the rot has been there for years.

I maintained the roof. That was my life's work. I patched and I reinforced and I argued with every man who wanted to tear out a beam because it was inconvenient. And I failed. Not because the task was impossible, but because I could not make enough men care about a roof they had never seen leak.

The general who has entered Rome — it does not matter which one. If not this one, another. The legions were always the force that the constitution could not answer, because the constitution was designed for a city-state where the army was the citizenry. We became an empire and kept the government of a city. That contradiction was always going to destroy us. I simply believed it would take longer.

You asked me once what happens when the commands are never returned. Now you know. This is what happens. A man with soldiers walks through the gate and the senate, that ancient body, that repository of every custom and precedent and hard-won compromise of four centuries — the senate does nothing. Because it cannot. Because custom is not a wall. Custom is an agreement, and agreements end when one party has the power to end them and the will to do so."

responses.playerDialogue: The weight of his words settles into you like stone into mortar.
You can hear the old man's voice in the words — tired, precise, sorrowful beyond measure.
Even from beyond the grave, Scaurus forces you to see clearly.
The analysis is flawless. The grief behind it is immense.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: You turn to the final passage.

marcus_scaurus_convo_2_n03.npcDialogue:
"I am writing this letter by lamplight, and my hand is not what it was. So I will end with what matters.

I do not know what you will build in the ruins of what I spent my life defending. I do not know if you will be a good man or a necessary one or simply a survivor. But I know that I told you the truth about how republics die, and I believe you understood it, and that understanding is the only inheritance I have left to give.

Remember that the Republic was never its buildings or its laws or its legions. It was the agreement. When enough men of sufficient character agreed to be bound by something larger than themselves, Rome governed itself. When they stopped, it didn't. Everything else is ornamentation.

If you rebuild, build that. Not the laws. Not the offices. Not the ceremonies. Build the agreement. It is the only thing that ever mattered.

I am Marcus Aemilius Scaurus. I was Princeps Senatus. I kept faith with the ancestors, and I failed, and I am not ashamed, because the keeping of faith is its own justification.

Vale."

options.playerDialogue: You fold the letter carefully and place it inside your toga, against your chest. Then you go out into a Rome that no longer belongs to the senate.

marcus_scaurus_convo_2_n04.npcDialogue: The slave waits, watching you. He asks if there is a reply. There is no reply. There is no one left to receive it.

deltas.reason: Carried the weight of Scaurus's final words
